Learning Objectives

Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you should be able to:

  • 01Explain how GNSS/GPS determines location, and identify major sources of positioning error.
  • 02Differentiate key GNSS applications and describe a basic field digitizing workflow.
  • 03Explain the basic principles of remotely sensed imagery.

Two ways to collect coordinates

Broadly, there are two common ways to obtain geographic coordinates:

  • 1In the field — measuring positions directly with GNSS/GPS.
  • 2Remotely — primarily from aerial and satellite cameras.

This week walks through both: how satellite positioning works (and why it’s never perfect), how we use it in the field, and how remote sensing turns reflected light into spatial data.

GNSS & GPS

GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System) is the generic name for a constellation of satellites orbiting about 20,000 km above Earth. They continuously transmit signals that let a receiver determine its 3-D position with global coverage.

GPS — the US system

GPS is the US-based GNSS, providing free positioning and timing worldwide. It was originally developed for the US military; after the 1983 downing of Korean Air Lines Flight 007, the US made GPS free for civilian use. GPS currently has 31 operational satellites.

Other GNSS constellations: GLONASS (Russia), Galileo (Europe), and BeiDou (China). Modern receivers often use several at once.

Trilateration & measuring distance

Trilateration is the mathematical method for determining a position using geometry — specifically, the intersection of circles (2-D) or spheres (3-D).

Trilateration vs. triangulation. They’re often confused: triangulation uses angles forming triangles; trilateration uses intersecting circles/spheres from known distances. Global navigation uses trilateration.

Measuring the distance to a satellite

The receiver computes each distance from signal travel time: range = speed of light × travel time. Satellites carry extremely accurate atomic clocks; your receiver’s cheaper clock syncs to them.

Why position is never exact

A receiver never gets a perfect distance — each range carries uncertainty (e.g. 20,000 km ± error). That turns each measurement into a fuzzy band of “where am I most likely to be?”

Group brainstorm
What real-world things might make your GPS position less accurate? Work it out, then raise your hand.
Range uncertainty accumulates from Instrument inaccuracy (satellite position error) · clock desynchronization (satellite and receiver clock error) · atmosphere / ionosphere delays · multipath (the signal bounces off buildings instead of arriving directly).

Components of a GPS

A satellite-positioning system has three parts working together:

  • ›Space segment — the satellites broadcasting signals.
  • ›Control segment — ground stations that track and correct the satellites.
  • ›User segment — your receiver (phone, handheld, survey unit).

GNSS applications

The main uses of GNSS are tracking, field digitizing, and surveying.

Tracking

Recording the locations of objects through time — a time series of positions answering “where has it been?” Receivers ride on trucks, ships, buses, boats, and animals.

Fixed-site monitoring

Receivers can also sit stationary: you record the site’s coordinates once, then monitor something over time — air quality (e.g. PurpleAir PM2.5), weather stations, or stream gauges.

Field digitizing

Field digitizing collects new or updated feature positions in the field with GNSS (points, lines, polygons) along with their attributes. A typical workflow:

  • 1Activate the GNSS receiver
  • 2Log positions at a set rate, or capture discrete points
  • 3Record attributes
  • 4Collect multiple features
  • 5Post-process (correction, remove bad points)
  • 6Export to a GIS format

GNSS is often used to directly digitize new control points — high-quality reference points used to correct and transform imagery or maps.

Field digitizing accuracy & surveying

Some things you can control, some you can’t:

You can controlYou can’t control
Equipment qualityObstructions and interference
Field technique (how you use it)Atmospheric conditions

If terrain or buildings rise above about 15° of the sky, you lose a meaningful chunk of usable satellites.

A range pole is one of the easiest and most effective ways to improve collection efficiency and quality — it lifts the receiver above your body and nearby clutter.

Optical & laser surveying

Historically, coordinate surveys used optical instruments — transits, theodolites, and electronic distance meters. Though slowly being replaced by satellite positioning and ground-based lasers, they’re still highly accurate. A modern total station measures angles and distances to compute coordinates: from a known point, it measures the horizontal and vertical angle to a target plus the slope distance via an EDM (electronic distance measurement) laser/infrared beam, then uses trigonometry.

Remote sensing: principles

Images are data recorded from a distance — remotely sensed data. They’re valuable for their broad-area coverage, extended spectral range, geometric accuracy, and as a permanent record.

Most remote sensing measures reflected electromagnetic radiation (EMR), and the principal source for GIS is sunlight. Light is characterized by its wavelength — the distance between peaks — with visible light spanning roughly 0.4–0.7 micrometers.

Most solid objects absorb or reflect incident energy (and transmit none). Different materials reflect different wavelengths — their spectral signature. Notably, near-infrared (NIR) responds to leaf cell structure, so healthy vegetation reflects a lot of NIR (which is why it glows in false-color imagery).

Passive vs. active

Most aerial and satellite systems are passive — they use sunlight reflected off the target. Active systems generate their own energy and detect what returns (e.g. radar and LiDAR).

LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) pulses a laser and times the returns — first return off the canopy, last return off the ground — to build detailed 3-D point clouds.

Aerial & satellite imagery

Aerial images

Images from airborne cameras are a primary source of geographic data. Modern aerial cameras are typically mounted inside an aircraft, pointing through an underside bay, capturing overlapping frames.

Satellite images

Satellites cover very large areas repeatedly. A few long-running systems:

  • SPOTOne of the longest-running mid- to high-resolution systems (since the 1980s); visible through mid-infrared bands at 10–20 m.
  • Landsat-9Latest in the longest-running series (since 1972): a 15 m panchromatic band, 8 multispectral bands at 30 m, and two thermal bands at 100 m.
  • SentinelESA’s six-mission system; 12 bands from below-blue through shortwave infrared, at 10–60 m (visible/NIR at 10 m).

What satellite imagery is for

Two primary GIS uses: creating and updating data layers (e.g. land cover), and detecting and monitoring change over time.

Key takeaways

1
Geographic coordinates are collected either in the field with GNSS/GPS or remotely from aerial/satellite imagery.
2
GNSS works because satellites broadcast signals that let a receiver compute 3-D position by trilateration.
3
Position accuracy depends on several error sources — satellite/instrument error, clock desync, atmosphere, and multipath.
4
Remote sensing measures reflected EMR; analysts work with wavelengths/bands, build composites, and map land cover and change.

Key terms & concepts

Open any term to see its definition and an example.
